panels.title: Upstream Errors by Type (errors/s)

options.content:
### 🎚️ Served Tip — deliberate cluster lag (which block we serve vs what's available)
eRPC's served-tip feature advertises the MIN block of the largest cluster of agreeing upstreams (monotonic, cluster-shared) instead of the single most-ahead one — trading a small bounded lag for far fewer 'block not found' retries. **Trust the SECONDS / BLOCKS panels** (served vs poller-freshest); the raw block-gap is an approximation. *(There is no direct served-tip metric yet — it lives only in OTel spans.)*

targets.expr: topk(12, clamp_min(max by (network) (erpc_network_latest_block_timestamp_distance_seconds{origin="network_response",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"}) - max by (network) (erpc_network_latest_block_timestamp_distance_seconds{origin="evm_state_poller",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"}), 0) > 0.5)

panels.title: ⏱️ Served-tip lag — SECONDS (trustworthy)
panels.description: THE trustworthy 'how far behind do we serve' number. Seconds between the freshness of the block we actually SERVE (origin=network_response) and the freshest block our poller has SEEN (origin=evm_state_poller). Both come from the same network object → no scrape skew, comparable across chains. Healthy = a fraction of a second up to ~1 block-time. Seconds on a fast chain / tens of seconds on mainnet = we're holding back real time on purpose (check servedTip.clusterDelta or a laggard dragging the cluster). Negatives are jitter (clamped to 0). >0.5s shown.

targets.expr: topk(12, clamp_min((max by (network) (erpc_network_latest_block_timestamp_distance_seconds{origin="network_response",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"}) - max by (network) (erpc_network_latest_block_timestamp_distance_seconds{origin="evm_state_poller",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"})) / on(network) group_left() (max by (network)(erpc_network_dynamic_block_time_milliseconds{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"})/1000), 0) > 1.5)

panels.title: 🎯 Served-tip lag — in BLOCKS (cross-chain)
panels.description: The same deliberate lag as the SECONDS panel, divided by each chain's block time → 'how many blocks behind the freshest we serve', comparable on every chain. ~1 = healthy steady state (serve ~1 block behind freshest). 2-3 = mild intentional cushion. 5+ = holding back more than expected — check servedTip.clusterDelta and the per-upstream spread for a laggard. >1.5 shown.

targets.expr: topk(12, (max by (network) (erpc_upstream_latest_block_number{upstream!="*",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"}) - on(network) max by (network) (erpc_cache_connector_latest_block_number{network=~"^${network:regex}$"})) > 1)

panels.title: 📊 Block gap: highest available − served (APPROX)
panels.description: APPROXIMATE block-count view of how far the served tip sits below the most-ahead upstream. Uses erpc_cache_connector_latest_block_number (the cluster/cache shared value) as the served proxy — there is NO direct served-tip metric (it's only an OTel span today). Two caveats: (1) the two metrics are scraped independently, so on fast chains most of a small number is timing skew; (2) cache may be keyed evm:<id> while upstreams use the alias, so some chains won't line up. Trust the SECONDS panel for the real lag; this is here only for the block-number view you asked for. Chase only big, persistent values. >1 shown.

panels.title: 🏷️ Latest block per upstream/vendor (pick ONE chain)
panels.description: One row per upstream+vendor with the latest block each reports for the selected chain (collapsed across replicas). Sort by block desc: top = furthest AHEAD, bottom = laggard. The served tip sits at/just-below the bottom of the largest agreeing cluster — so a provider far below the pack is an outlier the picker correctly isn't waiting for. ⚠ Pick ONE chain in $network or block numbers from different chains get mixed.

targets.expr: max by (network, vendor, upstream) (erpc_upstream_latest_block_number{upstream!="*",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$"})

panels.description: Three lines for the selected chain over time: HIGHEST available (most-ahead upstream), LOWEST available (laggiest), and the SERVED tip (cache proxy). Healthy = a tight parallel band all climbing together, served riding just below highest, never dipping below lowest, never going backwards (served tip is monotonic — only a >1024-block reorg rolls it back). Watch for: served flat-lining while highest climbs (stuck tip), or the band fanning wide (upstreams diverging → bigger deliberate lag). ⚠ Pick ONE chain. Served line may be missing for evm:<id>-keyed chains (use the SECONDS panel instead).

panels.title: Score per Upstream
panels.description: `sortByScore` output, HIGHER = better. Score = overall(u) / (1 + Σ metric×weight). Per-upstream `routing.scoreMultipliers.overall` boosts visibly lift the line.

panels.title: Position per Upstream
panels.description: Current rank in the order. 0 = primary, 1+ = backup, -1 = excluded. Averaged across instances; a value like 0.4 means the upstream is primary on 40% of instances.

panels.title: Currently Stuck-Excluded (>60s)
panels.description: Upstreams continuously excluded for >60s. Alert on >600s for 'stuck out >10 min'. Filter by $upstream/$network to drill in.

panels.title: Exclusion Rate by Reason
panels.description: `excludeIf` trips per second, per leaf-predicate slug (`error_rate_above`, `latency_p95_above`, `block_head_lag_above`, ...). Option-(c) attribution: compound predicates emit one increment per leaf.

targets.expr: topk(15, max by (network, upstream) (erpc_selection_excluded_seconds{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}))

panels.title: Top Excluded (current duration)
panels.description: Top-15 upstreams by how long they are CURRENTLY continuously excluded (erpc_selection_excluded_seconds, wall-clock). 0 = in rotation. This is duration, not event count — pair with the 'Active Primary Upstream' timeline to see the exclusion windows.

panels.title: Exclusions by Upstream & Reason
panels.description:
Per-upstream exclusion rate, by leaf-reason (top-20 series). Tells you exactly WHICH upstream is being dropped for WHICH rule (`error_rate_above`, `latency_p70_deviation_above`, `block_head_lag_above`, ...). Drives root-cause: an upstream spiking on `error_rate_above` is a different problem from one spiking on `throttle_rate_above`.

**Reading the y-axis**: exclusion events per second across all eRPC instances (sum of per-instance × per-tick increments where this upstream tripped this rule). With N instances × 1Hz eval interval, a sustained N events/sec means every instance excludes this upstream every tick (always tripping); 1 event/sec means roughly 1 in N instances excludes per tick (intermittent / borderline).

panels.title: Primary Switches per Network
panels.description: Rate of primary-upstream changes per network. Sustained high rate = the policy is flapping (close-scoring contenders). Consider tightening `stickyPrimary.hysteresis` for that network.

panels.title: Sticky Holds per Upstream
panels.description: Ticks where `stickyPrimary` ACTIVELY held the incumbent against a challenger. High count = sticky is doing real anti-flap work.

panels.title: Readmit Age Distribution
panels.description: How long upstreams were out before being readmitted. Should peak near `readmitExcluded.reAdmitAfter` (90s in the default). Anything in the tail = stuck excluded longer than intended.

panels.title: Shadow Exclusions by Reason (dry-run signal)
panels.description: `shadowExcludeIf` trips — upstreams that WOULD have been excluded if the rule were real. Compare to `selection_exclusion_total` to decide when to flip a shadow rule to a real `excludeIf`.

panels.title: Cordon Events (admin RPC)
panels.description: Manual `erpc_cordonUpstream` / `erpc_uncordonUpstream` admin RPC events. Action: `cordon` / `uncordon`. Spikes = operator intervention.

}[1m])) by (network, upstream, category, finality, response_type, larger_than_consensus) > 0

options.content:
### 🔭 Tip of chain — *is it halted, slow, or lagging?* → *why*
Top row = the SYMPTOM per network (🛑 stale age · 🐌 read p95 · 📉 primary lag). Below = the CULPRIT (errors → cause, catch-up cost).

targets.expr: topk(8, (max by (network) (erpc_network_latest_block_timestamp_distance_seconds{origin="network_response",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"})) / on(network) group_left() (max by (network)(erpc_network_dynamic_block_time_milliseconds{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"})/1000) > 2)

panels.title: 🛑 Halted/stale — how many BLOCKS old our served tip is
panels.description: How OLD the latest block we hand to callers is, measured in chain blocks. Normal ≈ 1 block (we just served the latest one). 3–5 blocks = the chain is falling a bit behind; 10+ blocks = looks halted on our side. Cross-chain comparable because we divide the age in seconds by that chain's average block time — so mainnet (12s blocks) and base (2s blocks) both look like ~1 when healthy. Hidden when <2 (the healthy baseline). Sort: worst first.

targets.expr: topk(8, histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, network) (rate(erpc_network_request_duration_seconds_bucket{category=~"eth_getBlockByNumber|eth_getBlockByHash|eth_blockNumber|eth_getBlockReceipts",finality=~"realtime|unfinalized",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval]))) and on(network) (sum by (network)(rate(erpc_network_request_duration_seconds_count{category=~"eth_getBlockByNumber|eth_getBlockByHash|eth_blockNumber|eth_getBlockReceipts",finality=~"realtime|unfinalized",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0.2))

panels.title: 🐌 Slow — chain-tip getBlock p95 (s)
panels.description: How long the slowest 5% of chain-tip getBlock* reads take, per chain (realtime + unfinalized). This is what 'fetch latest' callers actually feel. If high → check the Tip culprit table (a slow upstream?) and the Catch-up Cost panel (waiting for fresh data?).

targets.expr: topk(10, max by (network) ((max by (network, upstream) (erpc_upstream_block_head_lag{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"})) and on(network, upstream) (((count by (network, upstream) (erpc_selection_position{method="*",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"} == 0)) == on(network) group_left() max by (network) (count by (network, upstream) (erpc_selection_position{method="*",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"} == 0))))) > 2)

panels.title: 📉 Lagging — MAIN primary behind freshest (blocks)
panels.description:
Blocks the MAIN primary (the upstream most of our replicas route as primary for method calls) is behind the freshest upstream eRPC can see. This is the real majority primary, not a single odd replica. Shown when >2 blocks.

WHAT IT MEANS: state-dependent method calls (eth_call / getBalance / getLogs at recent or 'latest') routed to this primary can return data up to N blocks stale.

HOW IT RELATES TO THE SERVED-TIP FEATURE: eRPC deliberately advertises a 'served tip' that may sit a little behind the single most-ahead upstream — it picks the bottom of the largest cluster of upstreams that AGREE on the head, trading a small, bounded, intentional lag for far fewer 'block not found' retries. That deliberate cluster lag is a DIFFERENT thing from what this panel shows. This panel = how far the ROUTING PRIMARY (where method calls actually land) trails the freshest available upstream — a selection/score/sticky outcome. The new 'Served Tip' row = how far the network's DELIBERATELY-CHOSEN advertised tip sits behind the highest/lowest available block, by design. So: a value here can be high purely because of routing (a sticky or higher-scored primary that happens to lag), independent of the served-tip setting.

NOT the same as 'Halted/stale' either: that panel is the AGE of the latest block we report (tracked from eRPC's freshest upstream), which stays fresh even when the routing primary lags. The three can legitimately diverge — e.g. fresh reported tip + low served-tip lag + high primary lag = 'reporting latest is fine and our deliberate cluster lag is small, but we route method calls to a primary that trails a fresher available upstream'. Worth a look only if state-read freshness matters for that chain.

PER-CHAIN: N blocks = N × block time — 13 blocks ≈ 4s on arbitrum-one but ≈ 2.6 min on mainnet. For the deliberate served-tip lag specifically, read the Served Tip row's SECONDS and normalized-blocks panels.

panels.title: 🔎 Tip culprits — critical errors → likely cause
panels.description: If the tip is halted, slow, or lagging on a chain (see the bargauges above), the offending upstream + likely cause shows up here. Each row = (chain · upstream · error kind) → plain-English 'why' in the 'likely culprit' column. Critical errors only — harmless ones (reverts/bad-params/catch-up-handled) are excluded. Sort: rate desc.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (network, upstream, error) (rate(erpc_upstream_request_errors_total{category=~"eth_getBlockByNumber|eth_getBlockByHash|eth_blockNumber|eth_getBlockReceipts",finality=~"realtime|unfinalized",severity="critical",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0.01)

result.text: ⏱ Timeout — upstream too slow (raise timeout/capacity, or replace upstream)
🚦 Rate-limited — raise limit or add capacity

targets.expr: topk(8, 1000 * (sum by (network) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_sum{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (network) (rate(erpc_network_request_duration_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval]))))

panels.title: ⏳ Catch-up cost — average latency added per request (ms)
panels.description: How much extra latency CATCH-UP (waiting for fresh data) adds to the AVERAGE request on each chain. 80 ms here means every request on this chain pays 80 ms on average to wait for blocks the upstream hadn't indexed yet. The bottom-line user-felt cost of tip freshness issues. Same number as the 'Amortized Overhead' chart below; this one ranks worst chains.

options.content: ### ⏳ Catch-up — empty/not-yet-available data forcing waits & retries (a top tip-freshness culprit)

panels.description:
How often eRPC retries because data wasn't on the upstream yet — broken down by WHY:

• block_unavailable — upstream said 'I don't have that block yet'
• empty_result — upstream returned nothing for a recent block (it hasn't ingested it)
• missing_data — upstream said 'I don't have this data'

All three mean 'wait one block, ask again'. A baseline rate is normal for tip workloads; a sudden spike on one reason → an upstream falling behind on that class. Failover retries (genuine errors) are in a separate panel.

targets.expr: sum by (network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_retry_attempt_total{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$",reason=~"block_unavailable|empty_result|missing_data"}[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: Catch-up retries — how often we wait & retry, by reason (retries/s)

panels.description: How long ONE catch-up wait lasts (per reason). Expect ≈ one block time for the chain: mainnet ~12 s, base ~2 s, arbitrum ~0.4 s — that's literally 'wait one block and try again'. If much longer, the block-time estimate is wrong (a laggy upstream is dragging the EMA). If pinned at 700 ms, the estimate hasn't warmed up yet (fresh pod). Bucket-honest now.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_bucket{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])))

sum by (network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_sum{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: Catch-up wait — how LONG each wait takes, avg & p95 (s)

panels.description: By Little's law, rate(_sum) ≈ the average number of requests CONCURRENTLY blocked in a catch-up wait. 4 here means about 4 requests are always sitting in a wait at any moment. Flat and <1 → negligible. Sustained climb → freshness problem on that chain. Slow chains weigh more (one 12 s mainnet wait = twelve 1 s waits), so compare per network, not across.

targets.expr: sum by (network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_sum{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: Catch-up pressure — ≈ requests sitting in a catch-up wait right now

panels.description: Of all requests we serve on a chain, what FRACTION hit a catch-up wait (because the data wasn't on the upstream yet). 1–2 % is typical for tip-read workloads. 10 %+ on a chain → many requests are hitting empties on recent blocks → upstream freshness problem, or RetryEmpty is too aggressive for that chain's response style. Counts retry events, so on chains with high emptyResultMaxAttempts the % is inflated up to the cap.

targets.expr: 100 * (sum by (network, reason) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) / on(network) group_left() sum by (network) (rate(erpc_network_request_duration_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Catch-up — % of each chain's requests that had to wait

panels.description: Total catch-up wait time spread across ALL the chain's requests = the average latency tax catch-up adds per request. 80 ms means every request on this chain pays 80 ms on average for catch-up. The bottom-line cost — pair with '% Affected' (volume) and 'Wait' (per-affected duration) to understand whether the cost is many fast waits or few slow ones.

targets.expr: 1000 * (sum by (network) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_sum{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (network) (rate(erpc_network_request_duration_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Catch-up — average ms added to each request

panels.description:
Catch-up split by what KIND of block was being read:

• unfinalized / realtime → tip reads. Catch-up here is EXPECTED — we're racing the chain.
• finalized → permanent old blocks. Catch-up here is WASTED — waiting one block won't make permanent data appear. Means the upstream genuinely doesn't have the data (incomplete archive). FIX: fail over instead of waiting, or replace the upstream — not a tuning issue.
• unknown → couldn't classify; triage like finalized.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (network, finality) (rate(erpc_network_data_unavailable_wait_seconds_count{network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Catch-up by finality — finalized waits are WASTED (red flag)
🔎 Other methods — issues to investigate (top critical errors → likely culprit)
panels.description: Same diagnostic table for non-getBlock methods (eth_call, getLogs, receipts, traces, …). Harmless errors (reverts/bad-params/catch-up) are excluded — see 'Upstream Errors by Type' for the full breakdown.

targets.expr: topk(20, sum by (network, upstream, error) (rate(erpc_upstream_request_errors_total{category!~"eth_getBlockByNumber|eth_getBlockByHash|eth_blockNumber|eth_getBlockReceipts",severity="critical",network=~"^${network:regex}$",project=~"^${project:regex}$",cluster=~"^${cluster:regex}$"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0.01)

result.text: ⏱ Timeout — upstream too slow (raise timeout/capacity, or replace upstream)
🚦 Rate-limited — raise limit or add capacity
